file_name: Ewees and Tawhid - 2025 - Multilevel thresholding for skin cancer image segmentation with velocity hunger games search.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses the Velocity Hunger Games Search (VHGS), a metaheuristic optimization algorithm (a form of computational modeling/AI), to perform multilevel thresholding for skin cancer image segmentation. It was evaluated on a dermoscopic dataset of 2,500 skin cancer images from the ISIC 2018 Challenge.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper introduces a novel algorithm, Velocity Hunger Games Search (VHGS), for multilevel thresholding in skin cancer image segmentation. It presents original experimental results, including a methodology section, evaluation on two datasets (a benchmark set of 9 images and a dermoscopic dataset of 2,500 images from the ISIC 2018 Challenge), and performance metrics (PSNR, SSIM, FSIM) comparing VHGS against other methods. The abstract and conclusion explicitly state that the study contributes by presenting an effective algorithm and demonstrating its superiority through experiments.

title: Multilevel thresholding for skin cancer image segmentation with velocity hunger games search
brief_summary: This study introduces Velocity Hunger Games Search (VHGS), a hybrid metaheuristic algorithm combining velocity updates with the Hunger Games Search, to perform multilevel thresholding for skin cancer image segmentation. The method utilizes Kapur’s entropy and Otsu’s between-class variance as objective functions to segment lesions from dermoscopic images. Evaluation was conducted on two datasets: a benchmark set of 9 images and a dermoscopic dataset of 2,500 images from the ISIC 2018 Challenge. Performance was assessed using Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), Structural Similarity Index (SSIM), and Feature Similarity Indexing Method (FSIM). Results demonstrated that VHGS consistently outperformed comparative algorithms, achieving the highest PSNR in 50% to 62% of cases and best FSIM in 50% to 87% of cases depending on the objective function. The findings confirm VHGS as a robust and accurate solution for dermatological image segmentation tasks.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion regions in dermoscopic images
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multilevel thresholding for skin cancer image segmentation. The Velocity Hunger Games Search (VHGS) algorithm optimizes threshold values using Kapur’s entropy and Otsu’s between-class variance to separate lesion pixels from background pixels, producing segmentation masks. Evaluation was conducted on 2,500 dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2018 Challenge covering melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis, using metrics PSNR, SSIM, and FSIM.

datasets_used.name: Benchmark Set
datasets_used.description: A small set of 9 images used for initial evaluation of the multilevel thresholding algorithm.

use.testing: Used for the first experiment to evaluate the proposed VHGS algorithm against other metaheuristic methods using PSNR, SSIM, and FSIM metrics.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image collection obtained from the ISIC 2018 Challenge archive, containing real skin cancer images with varying sizes and visual characteristics.

use.testing: Used for the second experiment to assess skin cancer segmentation performance using Otsu and Kapur objective functions.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine whether a significant difference exists between the proposed VHGS algorithm and other comparative algorithms across various metrics (PSNR, SSIM, FSIM, and Time) and threshold levels.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in performance between the proposed VHGS algorithm and the comparative algorithms.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in performance between the proposed VHGS algorithm and the comparative algorithms.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The test was used to determine whether a significant difference exists between VHGS and other algorithms. Results indicated significant differences in most cases, particularly at higher threshold levels, with VHGS showing significant superiority in 81%, 68%, and 56% of cases against DE, MPA, and SOA respectively.

Benchmark Set (Experiment 1).description: Evaluation on 9 images (DS001-DS009) using Kapur’s entropy and Otsu’s between-class variance objective functions across threshold levels 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, and 12.

VHGS.PSNR_summary: Achieved highest PSNR in 50% to 62% of cases depending on threshold level and objective function. Consistently ranked first in average PSNR values for Kapur method.
VHGS.SSIM_summary: Ranked first in average SSIM values for Kapur method. Maintained top rank at most thresholds.
VHGS.FSIM_summary: Ranked best in 50% to 87% of evaluations depending on objective function. Achieved top rank at most thresholds for Kapur method.
VHGS.statistical_significance: Showed significant difference in 78%, 75%, 59%, and 53% of cases against SOA, HGS, MPA, and SSA respectively (Kapur method).

PSO.PSNR_summary: Second-best results in many levels; highest PSNR in some cases.
PSO.SSIM_summary: Close contender to VHGS, frequently securing second position.

SSA.PSNR_summary: Secured third position in many levels.
SSA.SSIM_summary: Fourth rank in average SSIM across all images.

GA.SSIM_summary: Fourth rank in average SSIM across all images.
GA.PSNR_summary: Trailing behind VHGS in level 3 for Kapur method.

HGS.PSNR_summary: Lowest performance across all images in some levels.

SOA.PSNR_summary: Least favorable results in some levels.
SOA.SSIM_summary: Performance influenced by Img-2 at level 5.
SOA.FSIM_summary: Consistently exhibits least favorable performance across various metrics and levels.

DE.Statistical_comparison: VHGS showed significant superiority in 81% of cases against DE.

MPA.Statistical_comparison: VHGS showed significant superiority in 68% of cases against MPA.

SCA.Statistical_comparison: VHGS showed significant superiority in 56% of cases against SCA.

Benchmark Set (Experiment 1).notes: VHGS consistently outperformed existing methods across various threshold levels. For the Kapur method, VHGS ranked first in average PSNR and SSIM values. For FSIM, it ranked best in 50% (Kapur) and 87% (Otsu) of evaluations.

ISIC 2018 Challenge Dataset (Experiment.description: Evaluation on 2,500 dermoscopic skin cancer images (melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis) using Otsu and Kapur methods.

VHGS.PSNR_summary: Achieved highest PSNR in 50% of cases (Kapur) and 75% of cases (Otsu). Consistently secured top position for majority of images at levels 5, 6, 8, 10, and 12.
VHGS.SSIM_summary: Maintained top rank at most thresholds.
VHGS.FSIM_summary: Ranked best in 50% (Kapur) and 87% (Otsu) of evaluations. Achieved top rank at most thresholds.
VHGS.statistical_significance: Demonstrated sustained dominance and robust performance.

SSA.PSNR_summary: Secured third position in many levels.

GA.PSNR_summary: Trailing behind VHGS in level 3 for Kapur method; sixth position at levels 10 and 12.

DE.Statistical_comparison: VHGS showed significant superiority in 81% of cases against DE.

MPA.Statistical_comparison: VHGS showed significant superiority in 68% of cases against MPA.

SCA.Statistical_comparison: VHGS showed significant superiority in 56% of cases against SCA.

SOA.FSIM_summary: Least favorable performance across various metrics.

HGS.PSNR_summary: Lowest performance across all images in some levels.
HGS.Statistical_comparison: VHGS showed significant difference in 75% of cases against HGS.

ISIC 2018 Challenge Dataset (Experiment.notes: VHGS achieved the highest PSNR in 50% and 75% of the cases based on Kapur’s and Otsu’s methods, respectively. For FSIM, it ranked best in 50% and 87% of the evaluations using Kapur and Otsu. VHGS consistently outperformed comparative algorithms.

architecture_details.description: A hybrid algorithm combining velocity updates with the Hunger Games Search (HGS) to improve exploration of complex solution spaces for multilevel thresholding.

conclusions: The proposed Velocity Hunger Games Search (VHGS) algorithm, a hybrid metaheuristic combining velocity updates with Hunger Games Search, effectively performs multilevel thresholding for skin cancer image segmentation.

VHGS consistently outperformed nine comparative metaheuristic algorithms (PSO, SSA, GA, HGS, SOA, DE, MPA, SCA, HBO) across various threshold levels (2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12) on both a benchmark set of 9 images and a dermoscopic dataset of 2,500 images from the ISIC 2018 Challenge.

In the second experiment using the ISIC 2018 dataset, VHGS achieved the highest Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) in 50% of cases with Kapur’s entropy and 75% of cases with Otsu’s between-class variance objective functions.
VHGS ranked best in Feature Similarity Indexing Method (FSIM) in 50% of evaluations using Kapur’s method and 87% of evaluations using Otsu’s method.

Statistical analysis using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test confirmed that VHGS demonstrated significant superiority over comparative algorithms, showing significant differences in 81%, 68%, and 56% of cases against Differential Evolution (DE), Marine Predators Algorithm (MPA), and Sooty Albatross Optimizer (SOA), respectively.

The study concludes that VHGS is a robust and accurate solution for dermatological image segmentation, contributing to skin cancer diagnostics by providing reliable segmentation masks for lesion analysis.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on metaheuristic optimization (VHGS) for multilevel thresholding rather than deep learning, which may limit its ability to capture complex semantic features compared to modern CNN-based segmentation methods.

Evaluation was performed on only two datasets: a small benchmark set of 9 images and the ISIC 2018 Challenge dataset (2,500 images). The lack of external validation on independent, multi-center datasets limits the generalizability of the findings.

The ISIC 2018 dataset contains a diverse set of lesion types (melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis), but the paper does not report per-class segmentation performance metrics, potentially masking performance disparities across specific lesion types.

Performance metrics used (PSNR, SSIM, FSIM) are image quality metrics rather than standard segmentation accuracy metrics (e.g., Dice coefficient, Jaccard index, Sensitivity, Specificity), making direct comparison with other segmentation literature difficult.

The paper claims VHGS has the shortest execution time among compared algorithms, but does not provide detailed computational complexity analysis or hardware specifications, which are critical for assessing clinical deployment feasibility.

The study addresses challenges such as hair, uneven lighting, and irregular boundaries in dermoscopic images but does not explicitly report preprocessing steps (e.g., hair removal) or how these artifacts were handled during segmentation.

No data leakage risks are apparent as the algorithm is unsupervised (thresholding), but the reliance on a single public dataset (ISIC 2018) raises concerns about potential bias towards the specific distribution of that dataset.
